content.text: Azure Arc for VMware vSphere projects an on-premises VMware environment into Azure Resource Manager without migrating VMs to Azure. The Arc Resource Bridge — a small Kubernetes appliance VM deployed inside the vSphere cluster — connects to Azure and exposes vCenter inventory (hosts, datastores, networks, and VMs) as ARM resources. Once connected, operators can create, resize, start, stop, and delete VMware VMs from the Azure portal, CLI, ARM templates, or Bicep, and can enable Arc VM extensions (Azure Monitor Agent, Defender, Update Manager) on individual guest VMs.

attrs.alt: Arc for VMware — Resource Bridge, vCenter, ESXi hosts, Arc VMs, Azure ARM resources

content.text: The Arc Resource Bridge is the single outbound connectivity point — it only opens HTTPS (port 443) to Azure. vCenter credentials are never stored in Azure; the bridge holds them in a local Kubernetes secret and calls vCenter APIs on behalf of ARM.
Arc for VMware vs Arc-Enabled Servers

The Arc Resource Bridge is deployed as a VM inside the vSphere cluster via the az arcappliance CLI. The process generates a Kubernetes configuration for the appliance, deploys it into vSphere using the vCenter API, and registers the ARM resource bridge resource in Azure.

# Prerequisites:
# - vCenter 7.0 U3+ with credentials that have Datacenter-level permissions
# - Static IP for the Arc appliance VM
# - Python 3.8+ and az arcappliance CLI extension

pip install --upgrade azure-cli
az extension add --name arcappliance
az extension add --name connectedvmware

# 1. Create the configuration files for the appliance
az arcappliance createconfig vmware   --resource-group $RG   --name arc-bridge-vsphere   --location eastus   --out-dir ./arc-vmware-config

# The wizard prompts for:
#   vCenter address, username, password
#   Datacenter, resource pool, datastore, network for the appliance VM
#   Static IP, gateway, DNS, NTP for the appliance

# 2. Deploy the appliance VM into vSphere
az arcappliance deploy vmware   --config-file ./arc-vmware-config/arc-bridge-vsphere-appliance.yaml

# 3. Create the ARM resource for the appliance
az arcappliance create vmware   --resource-group $RG   --name arc-bridge-vsphere   --location eastus   --kubeconfig ./arc-vmware-config/arc-bridge-vsphere-kubeconfig

# 4. Verify appliance is Running
az arcappliance show   --resource-group $RG   --name arc-bridge-vsphere   --query "{status:status,k8sVersion:properties.version}"

# 5. Connect vCenter to Azure (creates the vCenter ARM resource)
az connectedvmware vcenter connect   --resource-group $RG   --name vcenter-prod   --location eastus   --custom-location arc-bridge-vsphere   --fqdn vcenter.contoso.local   --port 443   --username svc-arc@vsphere.local   --password "$VCENTER_PASSWORD"

Once the vCenter is connected, the VMs appear in the Azure portal as ConnectedVMwarevSphere resources but are not yet fully Arc-managed at the guest OS level. Enabling guest management installs azcmagent inside the guest VM and creates a HybridCompute/machines ARM resource, unlocking VM extensions.

# List VMware VMs visible through the connected vCenter
az connectedvmware vm list   --resource-group $RG   --query "[].{name:name, powerState:properties.powerState, osType:properties.osProfile.osType}"

# Enable Arc guest management on a specific VM (installs azcmagent inside the VM)
az connectedvmware vm guest-agent enable   --resource-group $RG   --vm-name "vm-linux-prod-01"   --credentials-username azureuser   --credentials-password "$VM_PASSWORD"

# At-scale: enable guest management on all Linux VMs in a resource group
az connectedvmware vm list --resource-group $RG   --query "[?properties.osProfile.osType=='Linux'].name" -o tsv | while read vm; do
  echo "Enabling guest management: $vm"
  az connectedvmware vm guest-agent enable     --resource-group $RG     --vm-name "$vm"     --credentials-username azureuser     --credentials-password "$VM_PASSWORD"     --no-wait
done

# Install Azure Monitor Agent on Arc-enabled VMware VMs (same as Arc servers)
az connectedmachine extension create   --resource-group $RG   --machine-name "vm-linux-prod-01"   --name AzureMonitorLinuxAgent   --type AzureMonitorLinuxAgent   --publisher Microsoft.Azure.Monitor   --type-handler-version 1.29

# Power cycle from Azure CLI (requires only ConnectedVMwarevSphere resource — no azcmagent)
az connectedvmware vm stop --resource-group $RG --name "vm-linux-prod-01"
az connectedvmware vm start --resource-group $RG --name "vm-linux-prod-01"
az connectedvmware vm restart --resource-group $RG --name "vm-linux-prod-01"

After inventory sync, Azure exposes vSphere resource pools, datastores, networks, and VM templates as ARM resources. New VMs can be created directly from Azure CLI, the portal, ARM templates, or Bicep — no vCenter access required.

# List available VM templates from vCenter inventory
az connectedvmware vm-template list --resource-group $RG   --query "[].{name:name, osType:properties.osType, memorySizeMB:properties.memorySizeMB}"

# List resource pools
az connectedvmware resource-pool list --resource-group $RG

# List datastores
az connectedvmware datastore list --resource-group $RG

# Create a VMware VM from a template
az connectedvmware vm create   --resource-group $RG   --name "vm-linux-new-01"   --location eastus   --custom-location arc-bridge-vsphere   --vcenter-id $(az connectedvmware vcenter show -g $RG -n vcenter-prod --query id -o tsv)   --vm-template-id $(az connectedvmware vm-template show -g $RG -n ubuntu2204-template --query id -o tsv)   --resource-pool-id $(az connectedvmware resource-pool show -g $RG -n "Resources" --query id -o tsv)   --num-cpus 4   --memory-size 8192   --admin-username azureuser   --ssh-key-values @~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub

# Resize an existing VM
az connectedvmware vm update   --resource-group $RG   --name "vm-linux-new-01"   --num-cpus 8   --memory-size 16384

# Delete the VM from Azure (also removes it from vSphere)
az connectedvmware vm delete --resource-group $RG --name "vm-linux-new-01" --yes
arcappliance deploy fails with permission error
Assign the built-in Arc VMware role to the svc account at Datacenter level
Appliance VM IP conflicts with DHCP range
Appliance VM gets wrong IP after reboot
Use a static IP outside the DHCP scope; configure gateway + DNS explicitly
Missing outbound HTTPS from appliance to Azure
Open port 443 from appliance IP to *.arc.azure.com, management.azure.com, *.blob.core.windows.net
Guest management enabled without VM credentials
Provide VM admin credentials; ensure VMware Tools is installed and running
Using vCenter FQDN that resolves differently from appliance
vcenter connect fails with TLS name mismatch
Use the same FQDN the appliance can resolve; verify DNS from the appliance VM
Check arc-appliance logs: kubectl logs -n azure-arc-appliance -l app=vmware-cloud-provider
Network Requirements for the Arc Resource Bridge

The Arc Resource Bridge VM has two network interface requirements: one for management traffic (HTTPS to Azure) and one for vCenter API traffic (HTTPS to vCenter). Both flows are outbound-only from the appliance. No inbound ports need to be opened on the appliance from outside vSphere.
VM disk I/O during template cloning

# Verify outbound connectivity FROM the appliance VM (SSH into it first)
# The appliance exposes kubectl access via the kubeconfig generated during deploy

export KUBECONFIG=./arc-vmware-config/arc-bridge-vsphere-kubeconfig

# Check appliance pods health
kubectl get pods -A

# Check connectivity from the appliance to Azure (run inside a debug pod)
kubectl run nettest --image=alpine --restart=Never -it --rm --   wget -qO- --timeout=5 https://management.azure.com/ 2>&1 | head -3

# Check bridge logs for vCenter connectivity issues
kubectl logs -n azure-arc-appliance   $(kubectl get pod -n azure-arc-appliance -l app=vmware-cloud-provider -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')   --tail=50

# Check appliance heartbeat from Azure side
az arcappliance show   --resource-group $RG   --name arc-bridge-vsphere   --query "{status:status,lastUpdated:systemData.lastModifiedAt}"

# Rotate vCenter credentials stored in the appliance (no Azure re-registration needed)
az arcappliance update-infracredentials vmware   --resource-group $RG   --name arc-bridge-vsphere

A common pattern for Arc VMware is delegating VM creation to application teams without giving them vCenter access. Azure RBAC on the Custom Location and the ARM resource types enables this. Teams can create VMs from pre-approved templates but cannot modify vCenter networking or storage directly.

# Built-in roles relevant to Arc VMware
# "Azure Arc VMware Private Clouds User" — list inventory (templates, resource pools)
# "Azure Arc VMware VM Contributor"      — create, resize, delete VMs
# "Azure Arc VMware VM Operator"         — start, stop, restart existing VMs

# Grant the app team VM creation rights scoped to a resource group
APP_TEAM_OID="<AAD group object ID>"

az role assignment create   --assignee $APP_TEAM_OID   --role "Azure Arc VMware VM Contributor"   --scope "/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION/resourceGroups/$RG"

# Also need read access to inventory resources (Custom Location scope)
CL_ID=$(az customlocation show -g $RG -n arc-bridge-vsphere --query id -o tsv)
az role assignment create   --assignee $APP_TEAM_OID   --role "Azure Arc VMware Private Clouds User"   --scope $CL_ID

# Restrict which VM templates are available using Azure Policy (deny non-approved templates)
# Custom policy example (deployIfNotExists-based):
# {
#   "if": {
#     "allOf": [
#       {"field": "type", "equals": "Microsoft.ConnectedVMwarevSphere/virtualMachines"},
#       {"not": {"field": "Microsoft.ConnectedVMwarevSphere/virtualMachines/templateRef.name",
#                "in": ["ubuntu2204-template","windows2022-template"]}}
#     ]
#   },
#   "then": {"effect": "deny"}
# }

# Audit: list all Arc VMware VMs with their template source
az connectedvmware vm list --resource-group $RG   --query "[].{name:name, template:properties.infrastructureProfile.templateRef.name,                cpu:properties.hardwareProfile.numCPUs, memMB:properties.hardwareProfile.memorySizeMB}"
Azure Update Manager for VMware Guest VMs

Once azcmagent is installed on Arc-enabled VMware VMs, Azure Update Manager can assess and deploy OS patches without requiring WSUS, SCCM, or direct RDP/SSH access to each VM. Patch orchestration runs through the Arc agent channel, so vSphere credentials are not needed for patching.

# Trigger on-demand patch assessment across all Arc-enabled VMware VMs in a resource group
az vm assess-patches   --resource-group $RG   --machine-name "vm-linux-prod-01"   --machine-type ConnectedVMwarevSphere

# Install patches immediately (production-use: prefer maintenance window)
az vm install-patches   --resource-group $RG   --machine-name "vm-linux-prod-01"   --machine-type ConnectedVMwarevSphere   --maximum-duration PT2H   --reboot-setting IfRequired   --classifications-to-include-linux Critical Security

# Schedule maintenance window with automatic patching (policy-based)
# Use Azure Update Manager "Maintenance Configuration" resource
az maintenance configuration create   --resource-group $RG   --name "vmware-monthly-patch-window"   --maintenance-scope InGuestPatch   --location eastus   --start-date-time "2024-04-01 02:00"   --duration "03:00"   --recur-every "Month Third Tuesday"   --time-zone "UTC"   --reboot-setting IfRequired

# Assign VMs to the maintenance configuration
MC_ID=$(az maintenance configuration show -g $RG -n "vmware-monthly-patch-window" --query id -o tsv)
VM_ID=$(az connectedvmware vm show -g $RG -n "vm-linux-prod-01" --query id -o tsv)

az maintenance assignment create   --resource-group $RG   --location eastus   --name vm-linux-prod-01-mc   --maintenance-configuration-id $MC_ID   --resource-id $VM_ID

# Check patch compliance status
az connectedmachine show -g $RG -n "vm-linux-prod-01"   --query "{patchState:properties.osProfile.patchSettings.assessmentMode,
            lastAssessed:properties.lastStatusChange}"
Monitoring Arc VMware with Azure Monitor

After installing Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) on Arc-enabled VMware VMs, telemetry flows to a Log Analytics workspace. Data collection rules (DCRs) define what to collect: syslog, Windows Event Log, performance counters, custom log files.

# Install AMA on an Arc-enabled VMware Linux VM
az connectedmachine extension create   --resource-group $RG   --machine-name "vm-linux-prod-01"   --name AzureMonitorLinuxAgent   --type AzureMonitorLinuxAgent   --publisher Microsoft.Azure.Monitor   --type-handler-version 1.29

# Install AMA on Windows
az connectedmachine extension create   --resource-group $RG   --machine-name "vm-windows-prod-02"   --name AzureMonitorWindowsAgent   --type AzureMonitorWindowsAgent   --publisher Microsoft.Azure.Monitor   --type-handler-version 1.22

# Create a Data Collection Rule for syslog + performance counters
az monitor data-collection rule create   --resource-group $RG   --name "dcr-arc-vmware-linux"   --location eastus   --data-flows '[{"streams":["Microsoft-Syslog","Microsoft-Perf"],"destinations":["law-arc"]}]'   --data-sources '{
    "syslog": [{"name":"syslog-ds","streams":["Microsoft-Syslog"],
                "facilityNames":["auth","daemon","kern"],"logLevels":["Warning","Error","Critical"]}],
    "performanceCounters": [{"name":"perf-ds","streams":["Microsoft-Perf"],
      "samplingFrequencyInSeconds":60,
      "counterSpecifiers":["Processor(*)\% Processor Time","Memory(*)\Available MBytes",
                           "LogicalDisk(*)\Disk Read Bytes/sec"]}]}'   --destinations '{"logAnalytics":[{"name":"law-arc","workspaceResourceId":"'$LA_WORKSPACE_ID'"}]}'

# Associate DCR with the Arc VM
DCR_ID=$(az monitor data-collection rule show -g $RG -n "dcr-arc-vmware-linux" --query id -o tsv)
az monitor data-collection rule association create   --name "dcr-arc-vmware-linux-assoc"   --resource $VM_ID   --data-collection-rule-id $DCR_ID

# KQL: CPU > 80% on Arc VMware VMs for past hour
# Perf
# | where Computer in (arc_vmware_vm_list)
# | where ObjectName == "Processor" and CounterName == "% Processor Time"
# | where CounterValue > 80
# | summarize avg(CounterValue) by Computer, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
# | order by avg_CounterValue desc
Arc Resource Bridge Upgrade and Maintenance

The Arc Resource Bridge must be upgraded before new capabilities become available. Upgrades are performed in-place by redeploying the appliance VM with a newer image — the existing vCenter connection and ARM resources are preserved. Microsoft releases new appliance versions monthly; appliances older than 6 months lose support.

# Check current appliance version and available upgrades
az arcappliance show -g $RG -n arc-bridge-vsphere --query "{version:properties.version,status:status}"

az arcappliance get-upgrades -g $RG -n arc-bridge-vsphere

# Upgrade the appliance (downloads new OVA, replaces the VM, preserves ARM resources)
az arcappliance upgrade vmware   --resource-group $RG   --name arc-bridge-vsphere   --config-file ./arc-vmware-config/arc-bridge-vsphere-appliance.yaml

# Monitor upgrade progress
az arcappliance show -g $RG -n arc-bridge-vsphere --query "{status:status,version:properties.version}"

# If upgrade fails and appliance is in Failed state: recover by redeploying
# (ARM resources are preserved; only the appliance VM changes)
az arcappliance deploy vmware   --config-file ./arc-vmware-config/arc-bridge-vsphere-appliance.yaml

az arcappliance create vmware   --resource-group $RG   --name arc-bridge-vsphere   --location eastus   --kubeconfig ./arc-vmware-config/arc-bridge-vsphere-kubeconfig

# Verify vCenter connection is restored after upgrade
az connectedvmware vcenter show -g $RG -n vcenter-prod --query "{status:properties.connectionStatus}"

Azure Resource Graph covers all Microsoft.ConnectedVMwarevSphere resource types. Use it to build cross-subscription inventory dashboards, identify VMs without guest management enabled, and audit template compliance across the entire VMware estate.

// All Arc VMware VMs with power state and CPU/memory
resources
| where type == "microsoft.connectedvmwarevsphere/virtualmachines"
| extend powerState = properties.powerState
| extend cpu = toint(properties.hardwareProfile.numCPUs)
| extend memMB = toint(properties.hardwareProfile.memorySizeMB)
| extend template = properties.infrastructureProfile.templateRef.name
| project name, resourceGroup, powerState, cpu, memMB, template, location

// VMware VMs without guest management enabled (no HybridCompute machine linked)
resources
| where type == "microsoft.connectedvmwarevsphere/virtualmachines"
| where isnull(properties.guestAgentProfile.status)
| project name, resourceGroup, location

// Count VMs per ESXi host
resources
| where type == "microsoft.connectedvmwarevsphere/virtualmachines"
| extend host = tostring(properties.infrastructureProfile.hostRef.name)
| summarize vmCount=count() by host, resourceGroup
| order by vmCount desc

// Arc appliances with status (detect degraded bridges)
resources
| where type == "microsoft.resourceconnector/appliances"
| extend status = properties.status
| extend version = properties.version
| where status != "Running"
| project name, resourceGroup, status, version

// VMware VMs where AMA extension is missing
resources
| where type == "microsoft.connectedvmwarevsphere/virtualmachines"
| where isnotnull(properties.guestAgentProfile.status)
| join kind=leftouter (
    resources
    | where type == "microsoft.hybridcompute/machines/extensions"
    | where properties.type in ("AzureMonitorLinuxAgent","AzureMonitorWindowsAgent")
    | extend machineName = tostring(split(id, "/")[-3])
    | project machineName, extensionType=properties.type
) on $left.name == $right.machineName
| where isnull(extensionType)
| project name, resourceGroup, location

Arc for VMware vSphere projects on-premises VMware inventory into Azure Resource Manager through the Arc Resource Bridge — a small Kubernetes appliance VM. Once connected, operators control VM lifecycle (create, resize, power, delete) from Azure CLI, portal, or Bicep without requiring vCenter access for day-to-day operations. Enabling guest management on individual VMs installs azcmagent, unlocking the full Arc-enabled server feature set: extensions, Azure Monitor, Defender for Cloud, Update Manager, and Azure Policy. The bridge requires only outbound HTTPS port 443 from the appliance VM to Azure endpoints, and vCenter credentials stay local to the appliance.
